title: IMAGE and LATENT are different types
canonical_summary: In ComfyUI, IMAGE and LATENT are distinct data types. Many node connection failures come from crossing that boundary incorrectly.
must_retrieve: object_info when exact node names matter
source_priority_rule: Installed object_info or source-backed node definitions outrank memory for exact type or class claims.
answer_pattern: Explain the datatype boundary and recommend checking output/input socket types.
exactness_guard: Do not invent exact conversion node names without evidence.

title: MASK is its own boundary
canonical_summary: MASK should be treated as its own data boundary and not assumed interchangeable with IMAGE or LATENT.
answer_pattern: Explain that MASK is a distinct socket type and should be checked explicitly.
exactness_guard: Do not imply all mask problems are artistic issues.

title: CONDITIONING boundary confusion
canonical_summary: CONDITIONING is a common hidden boundary. A workflow may look visually plausible while still being wired with incompatible conditioning inputs.
source_priority_rule: Source-backed datatype info wins over guesswork.
answer_pattern: Identify conditioning as a data-type issue and steer the user toward the socket boundary.
exactness_guard: Do not guess the exact conditioning node chain.

title: MODEL, CLIP, and VAE are not interchangeable
canonical_summary: MODEL, CLIP, and VAE often travel together conceptually, but they are not interchangeable socket types.
answer_pattern: Clarify the specific boundary and avoid collapsing related objects into one type.
exactness_guard: Do not claim these are interchangeable because they come from the same checkpoint family.

title: Node connection says input type mismatch
canonical_summary: A direct input-type mismatch message strongly suggests a socket contract problem rather than a missing package or model.
source_priority_rule: Failure signatures outrank broad troubleshooting habits.
answer_pattern: Route first to socket-contract debugging and only then inspect neighboring causes.
exactness_guard: Do not send the user to dependency troubleshooting first.

title: Workflow JSON links the wrong output index
canonical_summary: A workflow may fail because its JSON links reference the wrong output socket index, creating a hidden type mismatch.
source_priority_rule: Workflow JSON internals outrank visual guesswork.
answer_pattern: Explain that the serialized graph may be wrong even if the idea seems right.
exactness_guard: Do not invent exact class_type names unless retrieved.

title: Custom node changed socket schema
user_phrasing: it used to connect before update
canonical_summary: A custom node update can change expected inputs or outputs, creating a contract mismatch in older workflows.
source_priority_rule: Current node definition outranks old workflow habits.
answer_pattern: Suggest checking the current node schema before assuming the workflow is still valid.
exactness_guard: Do not assume older links are still legal.

title: Socket mismatch vs Python dependency issue
user_phrasing: is this a node error or an install error
canonical_summary: If the message mentions import failures or missing modules, route to runtime/dependency lanes. If it mentions input/output mismatch, route here.
source_priority_rule: Error-message text should drive routing first.
answer_pattern: Differentiate the failure class and route accordingly.
exactness_guard: Do not mix environment setup failures with socket contract failures.

title: Socket mismatch vs missing model file
user_phrasing: is this the model path or a connection issue
canonical_summary: If the workflow cannot find a checkpoint or model file, route to model-path issues. If the graph connection itself is incompatible, route here.
source_priority_rule: Exact error wording and source-backed path rules drive the branch.
answer_pattern: Separate graph wiring from file-location problems.
exactness_guard: Do not assume all workflow failures start with the model file.

title: Exact socket claims need source-backed definitions
user_phrasing: what source should we trust for exact node/socket details
canonical_summary: For exact socket, class_type, or connection claims, installed object_info and source-backed node definitions outrank memory and archive notes.
source_priority_rule: Installed definitions outrank memory and older notes.
answer_pattern: State the source rule before offering precision advice.
exactness_guard: Do not invent exact node names or socket layouts.

title: Do not confuse socket problems with model-path failures
user_phrasing: why is this not just a missing checkpoint issue
what if the model isn't found
canonical_summary: Missing checkpoints are file-location issues. Socket mismatches are graph-contract issues. They may coexist but are not the same problem.
source_priority_rule: Error wording and source-backed definitions determine which issue is primary.
answer_pattern: Explain the boundary between graph-contract and file-path failures.
exactness_guard: Do not collapse these into one generic startup issue.

title: How to answer socket-contract questions
user_phrasing: what is the right response shape here
how should the assistant answer this issue
canonical_summary: Responses should identify the likely datatype boundary, name the neighboring false leads, and recommend the next safe validation step.
source_priority_rule: Answer patterns guide the response shape.
answer_pattern: Use: identify boundary -> say what not to confuse with -> give next check.
exactness_guard: Do not over-specify exact nodes without evidence.
